# Carbon Credits, Continued Deforestation: Satellite Data Exposes Cracks in Amazon's Flagship REDD+ Scheme

**Nearly 8,000 hectares of forest have been lost inside a protected carbon credit zone in the Brazilian Amazon since the project launched — raising serious questions about whether credits sold from this land represent real climate protection.**

The Pacajai REDD+ project in Portel-Pará, registered with Verra and covering 148,975 hectares of the Brazilian Amazon, was designed to stop deforestation. But satellite data analyzed by CarbonVerifier tells a more complicated story — one of persistent, ongoing forest loss across more than a decade of supposed protection.

## What the Satellites Show

The project covers a registered polygon of 123,276 hectares of forest as measured in 2000. Before the project's official start date of January 1, 2009, just 1,459 hectares had been lost over the preceding eight years — a relatively modest toll. Then the project launched.

In the years since, 7,963 hectares have been cleared inside the project boundary — more than five times the pre-project loss figure. That represents 6.5% of the forest that existed here at the turn of the millennium, gone on the watch of a mechanism whose entire purpose is forest preservation.

The year-by-year breakdown is striking in its consistency. Loss began almost immediately after the project's start, with 582 hectares cleared in 2009 alone — the single highest annual figure of the pre-project era by a wide margin. It continued without interruption through every subsequent year on record. The worst years were 2016 and 2017, when 998.8 and 984.7 hectares respectively were cleared — together nearly 2,000 hectares in just 24 months. Even 2023, the most recent year with complete data, recorded 828.5 hectares of loss, suggesting no trajectory of improvement.

There is no single year in the project's 15-year lifespan in which forest loss inside the boundary fell to zero.

## The NDVI Picture

Sentinel-2 satellite imagery introduces a layer of ambiguity. NDVI vegetation index readings from cloud-masked scenes between 2018 and 2024 show high overall forest cover, with forest classifications ranging from 96.1% to 99.7% across the four available observation points. The mean NDVI scores fluctuate between 0.765 and 0.827, suggesting that the majority of the registered area retains intact canopy.

The NDVI data and the Hansen forest-loss data are not necessarily contradictory — the former captures a snapshot of standing forest at a given moment, while the latter accumulates incremental clearance over time. Together, they suggest a landscape that remains predominantly forested but has experienced thousands of hectares of persistent, year-on-year attrition.

## The Additionality Problem

Perhaps the most critical question for any REDD+ project is additionality: would the forest have been lost anyway without the intervention? The comparison against control areas — nearby unprotected land at the same latitude — returns a verdict that CarbonVerifier's analysis labels "WEAK."

The project's post-start loss rate of 6.46% is lower than the mean control-area loss rate of 9.98%, a difference of 3.52 percentage points. On its face, that suggests some protective effect. But the control areas tell a deeply uneven story. Two of the five comparison zones — Control 1 and Control 3 — recorded loss rates of just 5.3% and 3.65% respectively, both *lower* than the project itself. Against those benchmarks, the Pacajai project performed worse than unprotected land.

The aggregate mean is heavily influenced by the three high-loss controls, which recorded rates of 12.13%, 11.34%, and 17.49%. The range across all five controls spans from 3.65% to 17.49% — a 13.84 percentage point spread that reflects the difficulty of constructing a meaningful counterfactual in a heterogeneous landscape.

## What This Means for the Market

No credit buyers are disclosed in the project's Verra registry metadata, meaning it is impossible from this evidence alone to trace which corporations or governments may have offset their emissions against this land.

That opacity matters. The voluntary carbon market depends on the premise that a credit represents one tonne of carbon dioxide genuinely prevented from entering the atmosphere. When nearly 8,000 hectares disappear from a protected zone over 15 years, and when the project's loss rate exceeds two of its five control areas, the accounting becomes difficult to defend.

The Pacajai REDD+ project may have slowed deforestation relative to some nearby unprotected areas. But the satellite record, as analyzed by CarbonVerifier, does not support a conclusion that it stopped deforestation — and in the voluntary carbon market, the difference between slowing and stopping is the difference between a partial mitigation measure and a credible carbon offset. Right now, the evidence suggests this project may be selling something closer to the former while claiming to deliver the latter.
